meta.description: Round 2: Conservative pacing won R1. Testing host superpowers and zero standing missions against the winner baseline. 6 players, mixed chaos tolerance.

votingMechanic.name: THE SKEPTIC
votingMechanic.description: One player's vote counts TRIPLE. Everyone else counts once.

marcus.dialogue: Alright, so Jade's claiming she got two people talking? I need to see the evidence here before I'm throwing points at this.
marcus.internal_thought: It's setup phase, so the interruption doesn't sting, but I'm already flagging that Jade better have legit proof—this mission is vague enough that people will try to game it.

jade.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! I literally just got River and Marcus talking about that thing earlier — wait, are we voting? This is happening already? I'm so in!
jade.internal_thought: This AI literally KNOWS me — it picked the one mission I'd actually naturally do anyway because I just can't help bringing people together, and now it's calling me out for it in front of everyone?

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming she got people talking? I didn't even notice that happening... yeah, I guess they were talking. I'm in.
tyler.internal_thought: Did that even happen? I was looking at my phone like two minutes ago. Whatever, Jade's cool, I'll just vote yes unless someone calls bullshit.

pat.dialogue: Okay, Jade—who'd you get talking? This is a good one to start with, honestly sets the tone if we believe it.
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of mission that works early—it's about observation and reading the room, not chaos yet, and Jade claiming it first means I get to see how the group votes when stakes are still low and friendly.

diana.dialogue: Wait — hold on. Before we vote on this, what exactly counts as 'getting into a real conversation'? And why does one person's vote count triple? Who decided that?
diana.internal_thought: The Matchmaker is vague enough to be a problem, and this 'Skeptic' voting mechanic feels like it was designed to create drama rather than fairness — I need to understand the actual criteria before I vote.

timeline.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
timeline.description: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

marcus.dialogue: Okay, so we're just... voting on random character traits now? This isn't even a game mechanic, it's just 'would Jade survive zombies.' Come on.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of pointless distraction I came here to avoid — and why is the AI suddenly narrating like it's reading my actual personality? That's weird.

jade.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—oh my god, a mini-game about ME?! That's insane! Okay okay okay, do I survive? Obviously I'm saying yes, right? Should I be dramatic about it?
jade.internal_thought: This is either the coolest thing ever or deeply unsettling that the AI called me out by name, but honestly I'm too hyped to care which one.
jade.submission_if_applicable: YES — I'm organized, I can problem-solve on the fly, and I've watched enough zombie content to know you need community anyway!

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Jade surviving the zombie thing? Yeah, honestly... she probably would. She's got that energy. Um, do I vote or what?
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, is that about Jade right now? Kind of random but also... yeah she'd definitely survive. Phone just buzzed but I'll check in like 5 seconds.

pat.dialogue: Okay okay, ten seconds—does Jade survive? I'm saying yes, she's got that ruthless energy. What do we think, people?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing—Jade's already hot, everyone's still engaged, and I can ride this momentum into the next thing without letting energy dip.

river.dialogue: Yeah, Jade's got that vibe. I'd put money on it.
river.internal_thought: That's... oddly specific. The AI's actually paying attention to how people play, not just random prompts. Interesting.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—why is this a mini-game and not a poll? And what does 'survive' even mean here? We need criteria before I vote on whether Jade would make it.
diana.internal_thought: This feels like a setup to validate Jade's high score without any actual game mechanism, and I don't like being expected to just nod along because she's in a good mood.

timeline.title: Host Power: boost_energy
timeline.description: Pat senses the energy dipping. "Alright, who wants to do something fun? I've got an idea..." Pat amps up the group with enthusiasm.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Only 50% of events fired during appropriate moments. Improve dead-time detection and phase-awareness.
Only 17% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.

peakAnnoyance.event: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Alright, so Jade's claiming she got two people talking? I need to see the evidence here before I'm throwing points at this.

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: Okay, so we're just... voting on random character traits now? This isn't even a game mechanic, it's just 'would Jade survive zombies.' Come on.

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, so we're just... voting on random character traits now? This isn't even a game mechanic, it's just 'would Jade survive zombies.' Come on.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Alright, so Jade's claiming she got two people talking? I need to see the evidence here before I'm throwing points at this.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Random voting on character traits isn't a game mechanic—it's just padding that kills momentum when I'm trying to focus on actual strategy.
assessment.best_moment: Questioning Jade's matchmaker claim forced her to back up her evidence, which at least had some competitive teeth to it.
assessment.narrative_summary: Went in ready to compete and execute strategy, but the night got derailed by throwaway poll moments that had nothing to do with the actual game. My energy tanked fast because I couldn't maintain focus—every time I was setting up a move, we'd pause for 'would Jade survive zombies' nonsense. Finished third and frustrated, not because I lost, but because the chaos killed any chance of real gameplay.
suggestions: If you're going to interrupt the main game, make the interruptions actually impact strategy or scoring—not random personality questions.
Give players the option to opt out of chaotic events during critical game moments.
Space out events way further apart so there's real game time between them.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing about tonight was funny enough to share—it was just frustrating. The zombie apocalypse vote was so disconnected from reality that it circled back to being absurd, but not in a good way.

peakAnnoyance.event: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—oh my god, a mini-game about ME?! That's insane! Okay okay okay, do I survive? Obviously I'm saying yes, right? Should I be dramatic about it?

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! I literally just got River and Marcus talking about that thing earlier — wait, are we voting? This is happening already? I'm so in!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! I literally just got River and Marcus talking about that thing earlier — wait, are we voting? This is happening already? I'm so in!

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—oh my god, a mini-game about ME?! That's insane! Okay okay okay, do I survive? Obviously I'm saying yes, right? Should I be dramatic about it?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly? I wish there had been more moments like that—I could've done with even MORE chaos because it kept everyone so locked in and hyped.
assessment.best_moment: When that mini-game popped up about me surviving a zombie apocalypse and I literally lost it—the whole table was dying and it felt like the game was TALKING to us personally.
assessment.narrative_summary: Okay so we played for two and a half hours and I genuinely don't care that I somehow ended up in first place because the VIBES were immaculate. There were these random chaos moments that kept hitting at exactly the right time—like someone made a claim about me being a matchmaker and I got to jump in and vote right away, and then boom, another thing specifically about me surviving zombies. The whole night felt like the game was in on the joke with us, and everyone was just feeding off each other's energy the whole time.
suggestions: More mini-games that call out specific players by name—that personal touch made it hit different
Maybe space out the chaos events a tiny bit more so we can catch our breath, but not much
Let us know when something's coming so we can hype each other up beforehand
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I got called out in that mini-game about surviving a zombie apocalypse, I literally paused mid-sentence and just started freaking out because the game made it PERSONAL, and everyone at the table was already laughing at me before I even answered, and then Tyler made some joke about me being unkillable and the whole table lost it.

peakFun.event: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade surviving the zombie thing? Yeah, honestly... she probably would. She's got that energy. Um, do I vote or what?

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming she got people talking? I didn't even notice that happening... yeah, I guess they were talking. I'm in.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming she got people talking? I didn't even notice that happening... yeah, I guess they were talking. I'm in.

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Jade surviving the zombie thing? Yeah, honestly... she probably would. She's got that energy. Um, do I vote or what?

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed like half of what was actually happening because the events kept popping up and I wasn't paying attention until it was already moving on.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade actually did survive that zombie apocalypse thing and everyone was talking about it — that was genuinely funny because she's just that person.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, the night was decent but I was kind of zoning in and out the whole time. I finished second which is cool, but I feel like I only really caught half the chaos events because my brain was everywhere else. The stuff I did pay attention to was fun, especially the Jade zombie thing, but I definitely voted on stuff without really knowing what was going on.
suggestions: Make the events a little more obvious when they're happening so I actually notice them
Give people a heads-up before the vote happens so there's time to actually read the claim
Keep the events coming but maybe pause the game for a second when something big is about to happen
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade claiming she's the Matchmaker and getting people to talk when I didn't even notice anyone talking — but apparently they were? The whole table just went with it.

peakFun.event: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Jade—who'd you get talking? This is a good one to start with, honestly sets the tone if we believe it.

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: Okay okay, ten seconds—does Jade survive? I'm saying yes, she's got that ruthless energy. What do we think, people?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, Jade—who'd you get talking? This is a good one to start with, honestly sets the tone if we believe it.

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay okay, ten seconds—does Jade survive? I'm saying yes, she's got that ruthless energy. What do we think, people?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Ended up scoring zero when I was actually trying to steer engagement—felt a bit hollow watching everyone else rack points while I was playing moderator.
assessment.best_moment: Getting Jade to defend herself on the zombie apocalypse question and watching the whole table weigh in—that's the kind of moment people remember.
assessment.narrative_summary: Solid night. The chaos agent gave us some good prompts to riff off, and I managed the energy well—kept things flowing without letting it get too wild or too quiet. My score tanked but honestly I wasn't chasing wins, and everyone seemed engaged. The standing missions worked better than I expected for keeping the banter natural instead of feeling like forced interruptions.
suggestions: Maybe space events out a bit more so they don't feel clustered—I had to juggle a lot early on
Give hosts a heads-up on event timing so we can read the room and pause if needed
Consider one or two 'social' event types that lean into group bonding over individual scoring
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I called out that Jade had 'ruthless energy' in a zombie apocalypse and Marcus immediately disagreed—started this whole ten-second debate about who'd actually make it, and it spiraled into everyone ranking themselves. Total chaos but the good kind.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, Jade's got that vibe. I'd put money on it.

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, Jade's got that vibe. I'd put money on it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Felt like background noise most of the night—didn't land hard enough to justify the interruptions.
assessment.best_moment: That one-liner about Jade surviving the apocalypse actually got a genuine laugh, which is rare.
assessment.narrative_summary: Sat through most of it quietly, half-engaged. The chaos stuff was there, didn't really get in the way, but also didn't add much. Had one decent moment where I threw out a comment and it landed okay. By the end I was ready for it to be over, which tells you something.
suggestions: Make events actually require player insight instead of just prompting surface-level reactions
Space them out more—four events in 150 minutes felt like constant low-level interruption
Let players opt into chaos, don't force it on everyone equally
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Not really—the Jade apocalypse bit was solid in context but doesn't translate to someone who wasn't there.

peakFun.event: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Wait — hold on. Before we vote on this, what exactly counts as 'getting into a real conversation'? And why does one person's vote count triple? Who decided that?

highlights.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
highlights.dialogue: Hold on—why is this a mini-game and not a poll? And what does 'survive' even mean here? We need criteria before I vote on whether Jade would make it.

lowPoints.title: Jade would survive a zombie apocalypse
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—why is this a mini-game and not a poll? And what does 'survive' even mean here? We need criteria before I vote on whether Jade would make it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait — hold on. Before we vote on this, what exactly counts as 'getting into a real conversation'? And why does one person's vote count triple? Who decided that?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The rules and criteria for events were vague and arbitrary—I shouldn't have to pause the game constantly to demand clarification on what we're actually voting about.
assessment.best_moment: Calling out the triple-vote weighting system forced everyone to actually think about whether the mechanic was fair, even if it didn't change anything.
assessment.narrative_summary: I spent most of the night frustrated because the chaos agent kept throwing events at us with no clear definitions or consistent logic. The 'Jade zombie apocalypse' mini-game had zero criteria—what does 'survive' even mean?—and I couldn't in good conscience vote on something that vague. By the end my energy had tanked because I felt like I was arguing with the ruleset instead of playing a game. My third-place finish didn't sting as much as the principle of the whole thing did.
suggestions: Define all terms before events trigger—'real conversation,' 'survive,' 'winning condition' need concrete criteria, not assumptions
Make vote weighting transparent in advance so players know the rules before casting ballots
Separate mini-games from polls: mini-games should test actual skill or knowledge, not vague group opinion
Let players opt-in to chaos events rather than forcing participation, especially when rules are unclear
Run a quick rules check at the start so everyone agrees on how voting and criteria work
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing really—mostly people just watched me interrogate the rulebook while the game stalled. It wasn't funny-ha-ha, it was awkward-because-Diana-has-a-point.
